steps.explanation: A margin of error applied to a sample mean produces a confidence interval. With mean $20.5$ and margin of error $1$: lower bound $= 20.5 - 1 = 19.5$, upper bound $= 20.5 + 1 = 21.5$. The interval is $[19.5, 21.5]$.

A confidence interval conveys that the true population mean is plausible within the computed range, not that it is guaranteed to lie there. Values outside the interval are not impossible — they are simply less supported by this sample.
The interval describes the mean of the population, not the range of every individual data value in the population.
choices: • Choice A: "It is plausible that the actual mean is between $19.5$ and $21.5$" — this captures the correct statistical meaning of a confidence interval. That matches choice A — A is correct.
• Choice B: "It is not possible" for the mean to fall outside — confidence intervals do not eliminate other values; the true mean could fall outside this range. Eliminated.
• Choice C: Every value in the population is between $19.5$ and $21.5$ — the interval applies to the population mean, not to each individual observation. Eliminated.
• Choice D: The mean is exactly $20.5$ — a sample estimate supports a range, not an exact population value. Eliminated.
